free_text: Inclusive measurement of (p,pi-xn) double charge
exchange reactions on bismuth from threshold to 800 MeV
Proton beams from 120 to 214 MeV at IUCF, from 188 to
481 MeV at TRIUMF and 800 MeV at LAMPF.
For the TRIUMF and LAMPF measurements the target was
made of a thin bismuth layer (0.8-2 mg/cm )
evaporated onto thin aluminum backing. Due to the
lower currents at IUCF, thick targets (10-45 mg/cm)
had to be used.
Two identical targets
were irradiated at each proton energy. The first
target was put aside for several hours to allow for
the short-lived activities to decay. The radioisotope
211At is in secular equilibrium with its decay
product 211Po which emits a distinct high energy
alpha (7.45 MeV). The yield of these alphas is
directly related  to the production rate of
211At. The second target underwent chemical
separation for astatine immediately after the
irradiation. The chemically separated astatine
activities were determined by simultaneous alpha and
gamma counting. Whenever possible, measurements were
carried out by both systems and the degree of
agreement was used to determined the reliability of
the two systems.
All gamma spectra were analyzed with
GAMANAL program, and alpha spectra were integrated by
hand. To determine cross sections, authors first
calculated the effective cross section sigma(211At)
for the production of 211At from the yield in the
direct target; then multiplied this effective cross
section by the ratio of yields 210-xAt/211At in the
chemically separated target of the same thickness.
Beam integration at IUCF was measured with a Faraday
cup, while at TRIUMF and LAMPF it was deduced from
the 24Na activity produced in thin aluminum foils
(1.85 mg/cm2) of the same area as the target.

211At is produced in two-step process: 209Bi+p -> He
+ ..., followed by 209Bi(He,xn)211At.
Target thickness for 800 MeV protons was 0.4-2.0
mg/cm2.
Cross section error was about 10%, although
in some isolated cases the errors were larger.
5% detection efficiency.
7% beam integration.
6% target thickness.
1-40% counting statistics.

The measured cross sections were summed up for each
bombarding energy to yield the observed cross
section. This cross section is not total, since
products lighter than 203At were not included due to
their short half-lives.
Data were
calculated from the results given in subs. 2-10.
Results are shown in Table III, page 258.
